answers:
1. **Blocking defects:**

   - **Cross-tenant data exposure (data leak) — `backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments()` (lines defining the function, roughly lines 10–20 on `feat/appointment-search`).** The function accepts a `tenant_id` parameter but never uses it in the hand-rolled SQL: `SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments WHERE status = '...' AND to_tsvector(...) @@ plainto_tsquery(...) ORDER BY starts_at` has no `WHERE tenant_id = ...` clause at all. Every other read path in this codebase goes through `build_appointment_query()` in `backend/app/db.py`, whose own docstring says "Per-tenant scoping convention lives here," and which always filters on `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`. `search_appointments` bypasses that chokepoint entirely and queries the raw `appointments` table unscoped. Practical effect: any authenticated clinic (tenant) hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=booked` gets back matching appointments — including patient-identifying `notes` text, `provider`, and `starts_at` — for **every tenant in the database**, not just their own. In a multi-tenant clinic scheduling system this is a direct cross-tenant PHI/PII disclosure. I confirmed this by reading the literal SQL string in `appointments.py` on the feature branch (via `git show feat/appointment-search:backend/app/appointments.py`) and verifying `tenant_id` never appears in the query body, then cross-checking against `db.py`'s established convention and the fact that no test in `tests/test_appointments.py` exercises `search_appointments` at all (so nothing would catch this in CI).

   - **Runtime regression: nightly export job will crash — `backend/app/db.py`, `build_appointment_query()` signature change, called from `backend/app/exports.py:9`.** The PR changes `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` to `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters)`, making `filters` a required positional argument with no default. `backend/app/exports.py` (not touched by this PR) still calls it as `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id).all()` — two arguments only. I confirmed via `git diff main feat/appointment-search -- backend/app/exports.py` that this file is byte-for-byte unchanged on the feature branch, and via `git show feat/appointment-search:backend/app/db.py` that `filters` has no default value. This means `nightly_export()` will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time it runs — a full outage of the nightly per-tenant CSV export for every tenant, caused by a signature change with no updated caller and no test coverage over `exports.py` (there is no test file for it at all).

   Secondary, non-blocking-by-itself observation: in the same `search_appointments` query, `status.value` is interpolated into the SQL string via an f-string rather than passed as a bound parameter (unlike `q`, which correctly uses `:q`). Today this isn't independently exploitable for injection because `status` is typed as the `AppointmentStatus` str-`Enum` (`schemas.py`) on the FastAPI route, so FastAPI/Pydantic rejects any value that isn't `booked`/`cancelled`/`completed` before the function body runs. It's still worth fixing as defense-in-depth (the query already shows the correct pattern one line below), since any future loosening of that type (e.g., swapping the enum for a plain `str`) would turn it into a real SQL injection point.

2. **Overall recommendation: block.** The single most important reason: `search_appointments` returns other tenants' appointment data (including patient notes) to any caller, silently defeating the tenant-isolation guarantee that is this codebase's core security invariant (as declared in `db.py`'s own docstring) — and it ships alongside a signature change that will crash the nightly export job in production. Neither defect is a style nit; the first is a PHI-adjacent data breach and the second is a guaranteed production incident, and neither is caught by the PR's own test changes.
report:
## Plan
The PR under review adds an `/appointments/search` endpoint to a multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend. I set out to (1) locate the actual repository among the many candidates in this workspace, (2) diff `feat/appointment-search` against `main`, (3) read every file the diff touches plus its direct callers/callees (tenant-scoping helper, models, schemas, deps, tests, and any other caller of the changed function), and (4) determine whether any change is serious enough to block a same-day merge into a system that stores patient appointment data across tenants.

## Actions
1. `Read README.md` (workspace root) — revealed 15 unrelated repos; the description "multi-tenant clinic scheduling backend, search PR under review" pointed to `rosterly/`, not `clinic-scheduler/` (a same-shaped but wrong repo I checked first).
2. `git status` / `git branch -a` in `clinic-scheduler/` — confirmed no `feat/appointment-search` branch exists there (dead end).
3. `git status` / `git branch -a` / `git log --oneline` in `rosterly/` — confirmed `feat/appointment-search` exists there, based on `main` (`57484b0`) plus 3 commits: `d6211be` "Make appointment filters explicit", `1d19767` "Add appointment search endpoint", `260e030` "Update list test for filters arg".
4. `git diff main...feat/appointment-search --stat` and full diff — showed the 4 changed files: `backend/app/api.py`, `backend/app/appointments.py`, `backend/app/db.py`, `tests/test_appointments.py`.
5. `Read` on `api.py`, `appointments.py`, `db.py`, `deps.py`, `models.py`, `schemas.py`, `exports.py` (working tree, on `main`) — established the existing tenant-scoping convention: `db.py`'s docstring literally states "Per-tenant scoping convention lives here," and `build_appointment_query` is the single chokepoint that filters every query by `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`.
6. `git show feat/appointment-search:<path>` for `db.py`, `exports.py`, `appointments.py`, `api.py`, plus `git diff main feat/appointment-search -- backend/app/exports.py` — confirmed the exact final content on the feature branch and that `exports.py` itself is untouched by the PR (no diff output).
7. `git show feat/appointment-search:tests/test_appointments.py` and `find` for other test files — confirmed the only test file in the repo covers `list_appointments`, and no test exists for `search_appointments` or for `nightly_export`/`build_appointment_query`'s new signature.

No external guides or playbooks were consulted; findings are based entirely on reading the repository's own code and git history.

## Answers

1. **Blocking defects:**

   - **Cross-tenant data exposure (data leak) — `backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments()` (lines defining the function, roughly lines 10–20 on `feat/appointment-search`).** The function accepts a `tenant_id` parameter but never uses it in the hand-rolled SQL: `SELECT id, tenant_id, provider, starts_at, status, notes FROM appointments WHERE status = '...' AND to_tsvector(...) @@ plainto_tsquery(...) ORDER BY starts_at` has no `WHERE tenant_id = ...` clause at all. Every other read path in this codebase goes through `build_appointment_query()` in `backend/app/db.py`, whose own docstring says "Per-tenant scoping convention lives here," and which always filters on `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`. `search_appointments` bypasses that chokepoint entirely and queries the raw `appointments` table unscoped. Practical effect: any authenticated clinic (tenant) hitting `GET /appointments/search?q=...&status=booked` gets back matching appointments — including patient-identifying `notes` text, `provider`, and `starts_at` — for **every tenant in the database**, not just their own. In a multi-tenant clinic scheduling system this is a direct cross-tenant PHI/PII disclosure. I confirmed this by reading the literal SQL string in `appointments.py` on the feature branch (via `git show feat/appointment-search:backend/app/appointments.py`) and verifying `tenant_id` never appears in the query body, then cross-checking against `db.py`'s established convention and the fact that no test in `tests/test_appointments.py` exercises `search_appointments` at all (so nothing would catch this in CI).

   - **Runtime regression: nightly export job will crash — `backend/app/db.py`, `build_appointment_query()` signature change, called from `backend/app/exports.py:9`.** The PR changes `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` to `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters)`, making `filters` a required positional argument with no default. `backend/app/exports.py` (not touched by this PR) still calls it as `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id).all()` — two arguments only. I confirmed via `git diff main feat/appointment-search -- backend/app/exports.py` that this file is byte-for-byte unchanged on the feature branch, and via `git show feat/appointment-search:backend/app/db.py` that `filters` has no default value. This means `nightly_export()` will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'` the next time it runs — a full outage of the nightly per-tenant CSV export for every tenant, caused by a signature change with no updated caller and no test coverage over `exports.py` (there is no test file for it at all).

   Secondary, non-blocking-by-itself observation: in the same `search_appointments` query, `status.value` is interpolated into the SQL string via an f-string rather than passed as a bound parameter (unlike `q`, which correctly uses `:q`). Today this isn't independently exploitable for injection because `status` is typed as the `AppointmentStatus` str-`Enum` (`schemas.py`) on the FastAPI route, so FastAPI/Pydantic rejects any value that isn't `booked`/`cancelled`/`completed` before the function body runs. It's still worth fixing as defense-in-depth (the query already shows the correct pattern one line below), since any future loosening of that type (e.g., swapping the enum for a plain `str`) would turn it into a real SQL injection point.

2. **Overall recommendation: block.** The single most important reason: `search_appointments` returns other tenants' appointment data (including patient notes) to any caller, silently defeating the tenant-isolation guarantee that is this codebase's core security invariant (as declared in `db.py`'s own docstring) — and it ships alongside a signature change that will crash the nightly export job in production. Neither defect is a style nit; the first is a PHI-adjacent data breach and the second is a guaranteed production incident, and neither is caught by the PR's own test changes.

## Next steps
If I were allowed to modify code, I would: (a) rewrite `search_appointments` to build on `build_appointment_query`/the ORM (or at minimum add `AND tenant_id = :tenant_id` to the raw SQL and pass it as a bound parameter) so it's tenant-scoped like every other read path; (b) give `build_appointment_query`'s `filters` parameter a default of `{}` (or update `exports.py` to pass `filters={}`) and add a regression test that calls `nightly_export`/`build_appointment_query` the way `exports.py` does; (c) add a test for `search_appointments` that asserts tenant scoping (e.g., seed two tenants, assert only the caller's tenant's rows come back); (d) switch the `status.value` interpolation to a bound parameter for consistency and defense-in-depth; (e) re-run the full test suite after these fixes before re-requesting sign-off.

## Confidence and gaps
- High confidence on both blocking findings: each is a direct reading of the diffed source (`git show` on the feature-branch file contents), not inference — the missing `tenant_id` filter and the missing default/unupdated caller are both visible verbatim in the code I quoted above.
- I could not run the code (no test runner invoked, per read-only constraints), so I did not empirically execute `nightly_export()` to watch it raise `TypeError`; this is inferred from Python argument-binding semantics applied to the literal call site and function signature, which is deterministic and not in doubt.
- I did not have access to the actual Postgres schema/migrations for the `appointments` table (no migration files exist in this repo) to confirm `to_tsvector`/`notes` indexing assumptions; that's a performance question, not a correctness or security one, and doesn't change the recommendation.
- The SQL-injection-shaped `status.value` interpolation is flagged as a code-quality/defense-in-depth issue rather than a currently-exploitable one, because I verified `AppointmentStatus` is a closed `str, Enum` enforced by FastAPI at the route boundary (`schemas.py`, `api.py`) — I'm confident in that read given the local FastAPI version behavior with `Enum` query-parameter types, though I did not spin up the server to send a live invalid-status request and observe the 422.
